original_abstract: Hydroponics: A method of growing plants using mineral nutrient solutions, in water, without soil. When the required mineral nutrients are introduced into a plant's water supply artificially, soil is no longer required for the plant to thrive.After a long lapse, the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU) is again in the news.Experienced observers are wondering if this is not a 'second coming' 1 : it certainly looks that way in South Africa, where four affiliated unions of the leading confederation, the ITUC-affiliated Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU), have affiliated to the WFTU and are pressing the Congress itself to follow suit. 2 Much of the discussion is framed in terms of a possible unity overcoming the last major split in the international trade union movement.The Presidential Council (the smaller governing body) of the WFTU met in Johannesburg in February 2012 on the invitation of the four WFTU-affiliated COSATU member unions.It was addressed by COSATU president Sdumo Dlamini who told the Council that 'we (COSATU) will be entering into a discussion about the type of a relationship we must have with the WFTU and how such a relationship can take forward our commitment to achieve unity between the WFTU and the ITUC'. 3he 11 th National COSATU Congress last September was addressed by both Sharan Burrow, general secretary of the ITUC, 4 and by WFTU general secretary George Mavrikos. 5aced with strong support within COSATU for an affiliation with the WFTU, and in the presence of its general secretary, Burrow struck a conciliatory note.She referred to 'workers in China and Vietnam with who we have a close engagement', and Cuba, 'where I am working to strengthen our engagement', blurring the fact that the ITUC has no 'close engagement' at all with the workers of these three countries but is seeking such an engagement with their State-controlled trade union organizations.They are among the few remaining labour structures inherited from the Soviet model, which no ITUC affiliate, including COSATU, would ever accept in their own country.With respect to the WFTU, she explained that, although she deplored the 'misinformation and misguided criticism' of the ITUC, neither she, nor 'any of her team', would ever criticize the WFTU, because they believed in freedom of association and, whilst unity was preferable, pluralism could legitimately emerge from this fundamental workers' right.'The last thing workers need is unions fighting' she said, adding: 'George, my door is always open to dialogue and I hope there will be a time when we can unite all the workers in the world'.Burrow goes on to say 'we do have a fundamental difference'.What is it?'It is not communism or socialism', because she is 'a proud woman of the Left'.The 'fundamental difference' is the attitude to the Arab popular revolutions, in particular in Syria, where the
humanized_abstract: Hydroponics: method of growing plants in a solution containing all of the necessary mineral nutrients in water as a medium in which to grow plants instead of soil. Artificially adding necessary mineral nutrients to the water in which a plant is growing therefore removes any necessity for the presence of the soil. After a long absence from the limelight, the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU) has suddenly reappeared in the public eye on a world-wide scale, leaving many specialists wondering whether this is not in the nature of a second coming — the original having occurred more than a half century ago in the context of the Cold War. 1In South Africa at least, that is what seems to be happening: four affiliated unions of South Africa's largest confederation, the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU), a ITUC affiliate, have recently joined the WFTU and are in discussions with COSATU on having it also join the WFTU, in search of unity after the long standing split which is said to have originated with the fragmentation of the International Miners Federation and the formation of the Proletarian Unity League of Russia in the 1920s. The Presidential Council of the WFTU held a meeting in Johannesburg in February 2012, at the initiative of the four COSATU member unions affiliated to the WFTU. The President of COSATU, Sdumo Dlamini, spoke to the council, stating that his organisation would start a discussion 'around the form of relations we would like to enter with the WFTU and therefore how we will promote the unity of purpose between the WFTU and the ITUC in the search for the ultimate goal: of bringing solidarity and unity to the workers of the world' 3On 10 September 2011, at the 11 th Congress of COSATU, the general secretary of the ITUC, Sharan Burrow, and that of the WFTU, George Mavrikos spoke 5Although the affiliation of four affiliated unions of COSATU to the WFTU and efforts for the rest of COSATU to join them seem to have a considerable amount of backing from sections of COSATU itself — and they were addressed by COSATU President Sdumo Dlamini who gave them an assurance that 'we will be discussing the form of relations we must have with the WFTU and how we should promote the unity of purpose between the WFTU and the ITUC for the final goal of unionising the world's workers in unity' 4In a situation in which the general secretary of COSATU was seated together with officials of the WFTU while cosatuan affiliates to the ITUC sought to affiliate to the WFTU in a unified body (in which a section of affiliates would belong to two different international labour organisations) Sharan Burrow stressed 'our close relations with Chinese and Vietnamese workers as well as Cuba, where I have already taken a number of steps to strengthen relations with the Cuban Trade Union' — while carefully and knowingly overlooking that for all these countries the ITUC has no relations at all with workers organisations: as is to be expected — given the principles of unions affiliated to the ITUC — this would be entirely unacceptable even to affiliates as progressive as COSATU itself. Instead the ITUC represents the official — and illiverged in the Stalinist tradition of trade unionism as labour organisations directly controlled by communist or so-called people's democracies — labour unions existing in China, Vietnam and in Cuba — inhersted from the post-1917 revolutionary period tradition. Burrow lamented the fact that there is 'so much misinformation and misguided criticism' of the ITUC, while — she stressed — she nor 'anyone on my team' would ever make any criticism of the WFTU, as believers in trade unionists' freedom of association. Workers, she said, do not want to see unionists having diverging or even opposite opinions, or — in other words — fighting one another: adding 'And in the future, George, my door will always be open for dialogue with the hope that one day there will be a moment when we all unite the workers of the world. We have however one major — rather fundamental — difference with the WFTU. Is it the communism or socialism, which the World federation, I presume, also affiliates to? I would not mind if I were that kind of a communist or socialist — I am already a proud woman of the Left and have never belonged to different leftist organisation — but in any case my affiliation to communism or socialism is neither here nor there in terms of trade unionism and I do not think that this difference in any way whatsoever differentiates us at this stage in terms of the principles which underpin our unions and our worldviews. What then — and where — is this difference?It is situated in relation to the movements of the popular revolts currently underway in the Arab world; and in particular, as regards the case of Syria — this whilst trying not to make things too obscure or confusing.

document.output: Hydroponics: method of growing plants in a solution containing all of the necessary mineral nutrients in water as a medium in which to grow plants instead of soil. Artificially adding necessary mineral nutrients to the water in which a plant is growing therefore removes any necessity for the presence of the soil. After a long absence from the limelight, the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU) has suddenly reappeared in the public eye on a world-wide scale, leaving many specialists wondering whether this is not in the nature of a second coming — the original having occurred more than a half century ago in the context of the Cold War. 1In South Africa at least, that is what seems to be happening: four affiliated unions of South Africa's largest confederation, the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU), a ITUC affiliate, have recently joined the WFTU and are in discussions with COSATU on having it also join the WFTU, in search of unity after the long standing split which is said to have originated with the fragmentation of the International Miners Federation and the formation of the Proletarian Unity League of Russia in the 1920s. The Presidential Council of the WFTU held a meeting in Johannesburg in February 2012, at the initiative of the four COSATU member unions affiliated to the WFTU. The President of COSATU, Sdumo Dlamini, spoke to the council, stating that his organisation would start a discussion 'around the form of relations we would like to enter with the WFTU and therefore how we will promote the unity of purpose between the WFTU and the ITUC in the search for the ultimate goal: of bringing solidarity and unity to the workers of the world' 3On 10 September 2011, at the 11 th Congress of COSATU, the general secretary of the ITUC, Sharan Burrow, and that of the WFTU, George Mavrikos spoke 5Although the affiliation of four affiliated unions of COSATU to the WFTU and efforts for the rest of COSATU to join them seem to have a considerable amount of backing from sections of COSATU itself — and they were addressed by COSATU President Sdumo Dlamini who gave them an assurance that 'we will be discussing the form of relations we must have with the WFTU and how we should promote the unity of purpose between the WFTU and the ITUC for the final goal of unionising the world's workers in unity' 4In a situation in which the general secretary of COSATU was seated together with officials of the WFTU while cosatuan affiliates to the ITUC sought to affiliate to the WFTU in a unified body (in which a section of affiliates would belong to two different international labour organisations) Sharan Burrow stressed 'our close relations with Chinese and Vietnamese workers as well as Cuba, where I have already taken a number of steps to strengthen relations with the Cuban Trade Union' — while carefully and knowingly overlooking that for all these countries the ITUC has no relations at all with workers organisations: as is to be expected — given the principles of unions affiliated to the ITUC — this would be entirely unacceptable even to affiliates as progressive as COSATU itself. Instead the ITUC represents the official — and illiverged in the Stalinist tradition of trade unionism as labour organisations directly controlled by communist or so-called people's democracies — labour unions existing in China, Vietnam and in Cuba — inhersted from the post-1917 revolutionary period tradition. Burrow lamented the fact that there is 'so much misinformation and misguided criticism' of the ITUC, while — she stressed — she nor 'anyone on my team' would ever make any criticism of the WFTU, as believers in trade unionists' freedom of association. Workers, she said, do not want to see unionists having diverging or even opposite opinions, or — in other words — fighting one another: adding 'And in the future, George, my door will always be open for dialogue with the hope that one day there will be a moment when we all unite the workers of the world. We have however one major — rather fundamental — difference with the WFTU. Is it the communism or socialism, which the World federation, I presume, also affiliates to? I would not mind if I were that kind of a communist or socialist — I am already a proud woman of the Left and have never belonged to different leftist organisation — but in any case my affiliation to communism or socialism is neither here nor there in terms of trade unionism and I do not think that this difference in any way whatsoever differentiates us at this stage in terms of the principles which underpin our unions and our worldviews. What then — and where — is this difference?It is situated in relation to the movements of the popular revolts currently underway in the Arab world; and in particular, as regards the case of Syria — this whilst trying not to make things too obscure or confusing.
